title: Stronger Minds, Stronger Communities
summary_en: This training course moves beyond simple awareness, providing youth workers with a robust "psychological first-aid kit" based on Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) principles, combined with holistic well-being practices. Our mission is to empower those on the front lines of youth work to identify early signs of anxiety, depression, and stress, while fostering resilience through a unique blend of emotional regulation and physical activity in nature. The 3 Pillars of the Program: 1. Mastering the ABC Model and cognitive restructuring to manage anxiety. Participants will learn to facilitate practical tools like "Calm Maps," breathing techniques, and positive anchoring. 2. Combining physical and expressive practices; such as acroyoga, mindfulness, and introspective writing to boost self-awareness and emotional regulation through non-formal action. 3. Building professional networks through Open Space sessions and empathy-driven activities. This pillar focuses on sharing NFE tools and fostering solidarity through group cohesion and nature-based exploration. This activity is specifically designed for youth workers, educators, facilitators, and youth leaders (aged 18-40) from Bulgaria, Italy, Greece, Jordan, North Macedonia, and Spain. The training follows a learning-by-doing methodology, to create a safe, inclusive, and highly participatory environment. Read all the information in the INFOPACK!
audience: Bulgaria, Greece, Italy, Jordan, Republic of North Macedonia, Spain

salto_specific.fee_text: This project has no participation fee.
salto_specific.accommodation_food_text: Accommodation, meals, two breaks, and travel are covered. It is a project co-financed by the EU.

title: GameIN: Gamified and Digital Methods for Youth Participation
summary_en: In this session, youth workers, educators, and non-formal learning professionals will discover how gamification can be used to engage young people in civic participation. The session will explore methods and examples of how gamified activities can encourage youth involvement in decision-making processes, while promoting democratic values and social responsibility. 15:00-15:10 – Welcome & Introduction. Overview of the GameIN project in numbers, highlighting key achievements and impact. 15:10-15:20 – Guidelines for gamified youth participation. Exploring key steps, best practices, and real-life examples for designing engaging and meaningful participation experiences for young people. 15:20-15:40 – Empowering Youth Participation: The Idea Competition & Concrete Solutions. How the Idea Competition promotes youth involvement in creating ideas that encourage peers to participate in civic and political life. Concrete solutions developed by young participants will be presented, showcasing the power of creativity to drive engagement. 15:40-15:50 – Gamie.org: Introducing the method bank and network of pioneers for gamification in youth work in Europe 15:50-16:00 – Open Discussion. A final interactive session to discuss the results, share feedback, and explore the next steps in enhancing youth participation through gamification.
audience: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Partner Countries Neighbouring the EU

salto_specific.accommodation_food_text: Free (online)
salto_specific.travel_reimbursement_text: Online

title: Methods Matter
summary_en: The training will specifically focus on methodologies and facilitation approaches used during Youth Exchanges. It will not address other key aspects of project management such as application writing, budgeting, administration or reporting. Instead, the course concentrates on what happens within the activity itself: how to design and deliver meaningful learning experiences for young people. Through non-formal education methods, participants will discover, test and analyse practical tools that support participation, intercultural learning, group dynamics and reflection. The course aims to provide concrete, adaptable methodologies that youth leaders can confidently apply in their own projects. Special attention will be given to the role of youth leaders as facilitators of learning processes, their capacity to manage diverse groups, and their responsibility in creating inclusive, respectful and engaging environments. While the course is accessible to participants with little or no prior experience, it is also open to those with some background who wish to deepen and refine their practices. Objectives: - To explore and understand key methodologies used in Erasmus+ Youth Exchanges. - To strengthen facilitation competences and group management skills. - To provide practical, ready-to-use non-formal education tools. - To develop skills to foster participation, reflection and intercultural learning. - To increase awareness and practice of inclusive approaches within group processes. - To promote peer learning and exchange of practices among participants with diverse levels of experience.
audience: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries

Art Well
summary_en: “Art Well - International Training on Art-Based Self-Care and Well-Being” is an international mobility of youth workers implemented within the framework of the Erasmus+ Cooperation Partnership project “Art Well”. This training brings together youth workers from Austria, Czech Republic, Ukraine and Hungary to explore and exchange art-based approaches that support self-care, mental well-being, and inclusive youth work practice. Through a structured programme combining non-formal learning, experiential activities, and professional reflection, the training aims to strengthen participants’ competences and equip them with practical methods applicable in diverse youth work contexts. This training focuses on using art as a tool for self-care, well-being, and inclusion in youth work practice. The training aims to: support youth workers’ own mental well-being and resilience, provide practical, transferable art-based methods for working with young people, strengthen participants’ confidence in facilitating creative processes responsibly, bridge research, local practice, and international knowledge exchange. FULL INFOPACK: https://www.canva.com/design/DAHA1Yc-7GA/Cbn_zoyYBGKbLl04VMaCRQ/view
audience: Austria, Czech Republic, Hungary, Ukraine

title: Participate! – Empowering Youth in Europe
summary_en: Youth participation in Europe is more important than ever. The European Union is under pressure from many directions. More and more political movements question the European project. After the 2024 European elections, a Eurosceptic majority in the European Parliament seems possible. At the same time, Europe faces urgent challenges. Refugees seeking protection put solidarity to the test. The war in Ukraine continues to strain societies. Internationals crisis like the war in middle east and ongoing uncertainties about tariffs and trade relations weigh heavily on economy and social cohesion. While this all dominate the headlines, the climate emergency is pushed into the background. In this multitude of challenges, Participate! focuses on what unites us: solidarity, democracy, and the rule of law. The project highlights youth participation in Europe as a key to building a strong and resilient civil society. We revive proven formats of dialogue between young people and decision-makers and develop creative methods that empower young people to implement local youth projects with European impact. We invite committed organisations, trainers, and volunteers across Europe to contribute their expertise, themes, and networks. Through a mix of interactive sessions, expert exchanges, field visits, and hands-on project development, participants will explore how to turn challenges into opportunities for meaningful youth participation. What to Expect 1. Identifying Challenges & Barriers Mapping the landscape: What obstacles do young people face in different European countries when trying to engage in civic and political life? Case studies: From bureaucratic hurdles to lack of resources—how do these challenges manifest in practice? 2. Sharing Experiences & Best Practices Peer learning: Participants will share their own experiences—successes, failures, and lessons learned. Toolkits & methods: Explore proven formats for dialogue between youth and decision-makers, such as youth councils,
audience: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries

title: Finding Your Voice - HU
summary_en: "Finding Your Voice" is 6-day residential training course promoted by Hangolda Egyesület (Hungary). The training wants to firstly explore the benefits of singing both as a youth tool and methodology. How can we utilise singing for promoting self confidence and developing positive self esteem and other youth work priorities such as teamwork, problem solving and cultural expression. We will then look to explore the power and influence of the ‘protest song’ and how we can use singing for promoting democratic participation and how, as youth workers, can we encourage the young people we work with to explore and engage as active citizens in the democratic process and find their political voice.

title: Hear Me Out. How to Use Participatory Audio for Supporting Young People
summary_en: Participatory Audio, one of the participatory media methods, is a way of empowering people by supporting them to share their personal life stories with the help of digital means of expression - in this case audio recordings, soundscapes and personal stories recorded digitally. The philosophy behind the method is that everyone has a story to tell. We often work with groups of people, who do not have a voice in popular media or are only represented by statistics. Many people believe that what they have to tell is not interesting enough, that they are not creative enough, or they don’t want to bother others with their worries and cares. We help people to tell stories which they are proud of, stories they want to share. We do that through a group process, where the participants are heard and respected, and where they themselves also learn to listen. We use simple and free tools, to keep the method open for everyone no matter what economic and cultural resources. We don’t expect a professional product in the end, and we keep the method flexible, to be able to adjust it to our different target group. During the activity we are going to use the “learning by doing” approach, which means that each participant will be invited to create their own podcast episode or a complete, short audio narrative to truly experience and understand the method. In the second part of the training we are going to focus on analyzing participatory podcasting as a method and teaching our participants how to become a facilitator and how to use podcasting and audio while working with youth with fewer opportunities. Although during this project each participant is to create their own podcast episode or an audio narrative we do not require any previous knowledge nor experience on voice recording or audio editing. This project is suitable for newcomers in the field. The main requirement is for participants to be open and eager to learn new skills. Who are we looking for. Participants profile: - Youth wo
audience: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Partner Countries Neighbouring the EU

title: Join our journey into “Minosia Labyrinth” – Learn and reflect on Migration and Inclusion!
summary_en: "Court ruling Italy must take migrants from Albania"..."Number of deportations increases by more than 20%" ... " Benefits for asylum seekers to be cut in Germany in 2025" ... "Greece with systematic pushbacks at Europe's external border" ... "New German government aims to deport more migrants". These are just a few headlines from the last few months on the so-called migration issue. Most of the measures are aimed at sealing off the EU, reducing the number of migrants and denying basic human rights. Driven by the electoral success of right-wing and populist parties and supported by large sections of the population, democratic parties in particular are pushing through rigid restrictions on migration. In this situation political education on migration and inclusion is facing the challenge to strengthen democratic values and empathy, break stereotypes and enable new perspectives and experiences. Our Journey to Minosia, which is an Educational Life Action Role Playing game on the topics of migration and inclusion, offers the opportunity to experience an innovative educational tool. Our four days training (+2 travel days) in a trans-cultural learning group let you step in a full-bodied learning process, that combines learning with hands, heart and mind. For whom? Anyone interested get to know a new approach on learning about migration and inclusion from the partner-countries Germany, Greece, Romania and Spain is invited to apply for our training. Please note that the Minosia Labyrinth method and facilities are not barrier-free, and English language skills are required. What to expect? Experience Minosia Labyrinth method in a 4-day immersive experience. You will learn and reflect on migration and inclusion through role-play and simulation. The method-toolbox provides a powerful tool to explore the complexities of the migration process and encourages participants to challenge their own biases and perspectives. Take part in the supporting program to connect you with the topi
audience: Germany, Greece, Romania, Spain

title: Training for Trainers - Immersion Lab n. 2 - Creative Methods in Interactive Performance
summary_en: This Training is part of the Accreditation of INCA. IMMERSION LAB is a training-for-trainers program designed to strengthen the capacity of professionals to use performing arts and participatory tools in educational and creative settings to foster inclusion. The course provides effective methods for applying theatre and movement as instruments for social intervention and artistic expression. Aimed at trainers, youth workers, educators, artists, and theatre-makers, IMMERSION LAB equips participants with the skills to design and facilitate creative processes with groups. Through collaborative and engaging performance practices, the program supports professionals in empowering individuals and communities through artistic expression and collective experience. OBJECTIVES 1) Strengthen trainers’ ability to design and facilitate creative, participatory activities using theatre, movement, and immersive methods. 2) Develop practical skills in physical theatre, clowning, and interactivity that can be applied in educational, social, and community contexts. 3) Enhance trainers’ capacity to use space, environment, and group dynamics to create engaging, meaningful learning experiences. 4) Promote collaboration, trust-building, and group cohesion through shared artistic processes and embodied practces. 5) Equip participants with adaptable tools and methods to support youth engagement, inclusion, and community empowerment through performing arts. PARTICIPANT’S PROFILE The training is open to youth workers, educators, trainers, and young artists including dancers, actors, performers, and creators of theatre or dance. Participants must belong to an organisation with an OID. This course is designed for professionals interested in developing their capacity to train others in immersive and interactive theatrical methods, and in integrating creative approaches into educational and community settings. title: The Polarities of Democracy: Learn from the Founder – Live Webinar with Dr. William Benet!
summary_en: This opening webinar sets the conceptual foundation for the course, introducing participants to the Polarities of Democracy framework—a practical approach to navigating polarities in democratic systems (e.g. freedom and authority, diversity and inclusion, justice and due process). In this webinar , participants will: - Understand the core principles of the Polarities of Democracy theory and its relevance in contemporary democratic contexts- - Recognise the 10 basic values that form the polarities of democracy theory and differentiate them from problems that can be solved - Explore the risks of over-focusing on one pole, leading to imbalance and democratic fragility - Gain introductory skills in mapping polarities, using real-life examples from civic and youth participation contexts - Reflect on their own practice as youth workers/educators and identify where polarity thinking can enhance their work - Engage directly with the founder of the framework, deepening their understanding through dialogue and Q&A The first online event will be on May 26 2026 from 19 to 20:30 CET.
audience: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Other countries in the world , Partner Countries Neighbouring the EU

salto_specific.accommodation_food_text: Online event.

title: Interference: promoting an European network for community radios on social inclusion
summary_en: Interference emerges from a shared interest in strengthening the work carried out by community radios managed by associations, as well as learning from similar initiatives in different contexts. On the one hand, the project aims to explore the potential of radio as a tool for social inclusion. On the other, it seeks to understand and develop innovative methodologies that foster active involvement of local communities. This contact-making seminar will bring together motivated youth workers with experience in radio practice, who are interested in improving their daily work and becoming part of a network of professionals sharing a passion for community media. During the seminar, participants will engage in discussions on social inclusion and community work, exchange best practices and innovative tools, and co-create podcasts. The programme will also include visits to community radio stations in Barcelona and to Ràdio La Víbria in Terrassa. Additionally, a booklet compiling information about all participating organisations will be produced.
audience: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries

title: Nature as a mentor: Shinrin-yoku in Youth Work Practices
summary_en: Nature as a Mentor: Shinrin-yoku in Youth Work Practices is an Erasmus+ training course that will take place in Dobczyce, Poland, from 12 to 19 July 2026 and will bring together 24 youth workers from partner countries. The activity responds to the growing need for more holistic, accessible, and preventive approaches to well-being in youth work. Across Europe, many young people experience stress, uncertainty, eco-anxiety, and low resilience, while youth workers often lack practical methods that can be safely used in everyday non-formal education settings. The training introduces shinrin-yoku (forest bathing) as an innovative, low-cost, and inclusive youth work approach. Participants will explore how nature-based methods can support well-being, self-awareness, reflection, relaxation, and healthy routines, while remaining clearly within the educational scope of youth work. The programme combines outdoor learning, guided forest immersion, sensory exercises, breathing and body-awareness practices, gentle yoga-inspired movement, journaling, reflection, group sharing, and facilitation practice. It also includes sessions on inclusion, adaptation of methods, outdoor safety, risk awareness, and the use of digital tools for documentation and dissemination. A strong focus of the training is on helping participants transfer what they learn into their own organisations and communities. During the activity, participants will design and test their own short nature-based sessions, receive peer feedback, and co-create a multilingual digital Forest Toolkit containing methods, facilitation tips, safety guidance, and inclusion adaptations. They will also prepare local pilot activities and peer-to-peer sharing plans to continue the project’s impact after the mobility. Importantly, this activity is not mental health training and does not provide therapy, diagnosis, treatment, or clinical interventions. It does not prepare participants to act as therapists or mental health professionals. I

title: Supporting CSO role in sustainable and inclusive development and in Global Gateway Investments
Cycle up: Methodologies and tools for quality Project Management and Human Rights Education
summary_en: At the core of our vision lies a firm conviction: multidisciplinary teams reach their full potential only when their members possess a holistic understanding of the projects they lead. This principle is the cornerstone of our upcoming training program. Learn more about us: IG @lpsdemadrid Are you an aspiring organizer planning your own training course? Or perhaps a youth worker looking to expand your horizons and master new professional tools? If so, this experience is designed specifically for you. In the field of Human Rights Education (HRE), youth organizations often operate with limited resources, which frequently prevents the luxury of having specialized teams for every task. More often than not, the same group of trainers must balance the creative art of facilitation with the rigorous demands of project management and administration. This reality demands a new generation of professionals: versatile leaders with a comprehensive 360° perspective. “Cycle up” invites you to dive deep into the intricacies of project management, advanced facilitation, and holistic leadership. Together, we will bridge the gap between administrative excellence and educational impact, equipping you with the tools necessary to navigate modern challenges and maximize the reach of your initiatives in today’s evolving landscape. We look forward to welcoming you to this transformative journey. AIM To equip 25 youth workers, leaders, and trainers with the advanced competencies in project management and facilitation required to elevate the quality of Human Rights Education (HRE). Objectives: To gain a better understanding of the concepts of Human Rights, Peace, diverse forms of violence, conflict, non-violence and conflict transformation with the approach to serve as a conceptual framework for HRE activities. To empower participants to join the HRE practitioner’s community by proposing new activities at the local, regional or European levels. To understand the basics of Non-formal education b

title: The Stage of Change
summary_en: "The Stage of Change" Zwardoń, Poland, 15-24 June 2026 (arrival and departure included), Training Course within Erasmus+ INTRO Contemporary societies are shaped by complex social dynamics, where inequality, exclusion, and power imbalances continue to affect individuals and communities. Young people often face situations of oppression—whether subtle or explicit—that they struggle to understand, express, or challenge. Traditional educational approaches frequently fail to provide safe, participatory spaces where these experiences can be explored and transformed. At the same time, there is a growing need for methods that foster active participation, critical thinking, and embodied learning. Many young people and youth workers lack tools to address conflict, navigate power relations, and engage in meaningful dialogue. This can lead to passivity, lack of agency, and difficulties in responding to social injustice. Participatory theatre methods, such as those developed by Augusto Boal within the Theatre of the Oppressed, offer an innovative response to these challenges. By transforming spectators into active participants (“spect-actors”), these approaches create a space to rehearse real-life change, test solutions, and collectively reflect on social realities. In parallel, there is an increasing recognition of the importance of body awareness and physical expression in learning processes. Disconnection from the body, stress, and lack of movement negatively impact emotional regulation, confidence, and group cohesion. Integrating daily body movement sessions supports not only physical well-being but also emotional integration, creativity, and presence. The Forum Theatre training course responds to these needs by equipping participants with practical, embodied, and creative tools to explore oppression, develop agency, and facilitate transformative group processes. Goal of the Project: To empower youth workers, educators, and participants with participatory theatre tools to exp

title: Keep it real! 2026
summary_en: Description This training course is designed for those with little to no experience in preparing a Youth Exchange application. Its main goal is to support young people, youth workers, youth groups, and organizations in finding partners and preparing an eligible Youth Exchange application. What does it truly take to prepare a REAL Youth Exchange application? What is YOUR REALITY that could be reflected in your project? If you’ve applied before, how was the experience—what challenges did you face, and what did you learn? What are the key tips and tricks for writing an application that enhances both the quality of the proposal and the future Youth Exchange itself? "Keep it REAL! 2026" will offer participants the opportunity to explore what it takes to write a solid Youth Exchange application. This course is primarily aimed at those with little or no experience in applying for international youth mobility grants—whether you've recently applied and received a negative response or are planning to submit an application for the first time. The main objective of this training is to help Youth Exchange applicants enhance the key quality aspects of their application process. Also, this training course will provide you with the opportunity to connect with potential partner organizations and begin developing a youth exchange project together during the course itself. The training approach combines theoretical input with hands-on practice, allowing participants to engage in real-life problem-solving, exchange advice, and tackle challenges that matter most to them. Erasmus+ Youth Exchanges involve multiple quality criteria, and this training course will focus on the aspects that applicants often struggle with: youth participation, equal partnerships, non-formal learning, intercultural learning, the Youthpass process, viewing the project as a long-term learning journey, and managing potential risks. We invite organization representatives to attend with a young person or young peopl

title: High Tides of Youth Participation (Participatory Seminar)
summary_en: High Tides of Youth Participation is a Participatory Seminar for organisations, practitioners and engaged young people who want to strengthen meaningful youth participation within organisations, local communities and European cooperation. It is rooted in the “high tides” many young people and communities are experiencing today: uncertainty, fragmentation and disengagement, but also change, energy and the search for new ways to take part in society. In this context, youth participation cannot remain a formal principle. It needs to become real, relevant and impactful. This activity will create a space where different actors can meet, reflect, learn from practice and build stronger connections for future cooperation across Europe. Aim of the activity The main aim of the activity is to enhance meaningful youth participation within organisations and communities. Objectives The activity will support participants to: reflect on what real and impactful youth participation means today, especially in times of uncertainty and youth disengagement; explore methodological frameworks, approaches and tools that can support participation processes; share effective practices of youth participation at local level, in communities and within organisations; strengthen the capacity of organisations to foster engagement, inclusion and successful participation-based interventions; lay the foundations for strong and sustainable partnerships within Erasmus+ Youth and the European Solidarity Corps, as well as beyond project-based cooperation; connect different experiences and perspectives that may inspire future high-quality cooperation and project ideas. Who is this activity for? The activity is designed for youth workers, project managers and coordinators, trainers, facilitators, youth participation practitioners, representatives of NGOs, as well as educators and teachers from the formal education sector, schools and informal groups active in the field of youth participation. title: International Seminar: Fostering Accreditation E+ Youth KA1
summary_en: This seminar will bring together accredited organisations responsible for managing projects under Erasmus accreditation in the field of youth and non-accredited organisations with relevant experience in youth work who are interested in further developing their approach and potentially accessing accreditation in the future. This seminar aims to get to the heart of accreditation: sInce Accreditation allows organisations to receive annual project funding without needing to describe each activity in advance, on a long-term framework, it means you can focus directly on creating strong, quality projects, fostering long term impact in your own contexts, deepening more focused methodoligies and enhancing project learning objectives. Who can join? This seminar is open to 30 participants from programme countries. Participants must: ● be representatives of accredited organisations who manage projects under Erasmus accreditation in the field of youth and... ● have a decision-making role in their organisation regarding the design, management and coordination of their accreditation activities: youth workers, trainers, project managers, international cooperation coordinators or... ● have an interest in understanding the Erasmus accreditation and in applying to obtain it in the future; ● be willing to share their experience and learn together; ● be able to work in understandable English; ● be available to actively participate for the whole duration of the seminar. Sharing, learning, improving The aim of the seminar is to create a space for mutual learning among organisations at different stages of their accreditation journey, where participants can reflect on the changes that the accreditation generates in their youth work. By making implicit practices explicit and transferable, participants will explore how accreditation can be further developed, adapted and strengthened in different organisational contexts. title: Strengthening El Salvador's Digital Civil Registration System
bOJA Symposium 2026 - Von Oida bis Online
summary_en: Achtung - Der Call ist lediglich für internationale, deutschsprachige Jugendarbeiter/innen offen. Die Anmeldung für Fachkräfte aus Österreich findet direkt über die bOJA-Website statt. Jugendkulturen sind mehr als Mode, Musikgeschmack oder ein „Trend“, der wieder vorbeigeht. In Jugendkulturen verhandeln junge Menschen Zugehörigkeit und Abgrenzung, probieren Identitäten aus, finden Ausdrucksformen – und testen Grenzen: im Kreis der Freund*innen, im Netz, im öffentlichen Raum und nicht zuletzt in Einrichtungen der Offenen Jugendarbeit. Genau dort treffen Szenen und Communities auf pädagogische Praxis, die bei den Lebenswelten und Interessen der Jugendlichen ansetzt und deren kulturelle Teilhabe zum Ziel hat. Fachkräfte der OJA bieten jungen Menschen eine Plattform für jugendkulturelle Ausdrucksformen und öffnen Räume für kulturelle Bildung. Die bOJA-Fachtagung 2026 nimmt Jugendkulturen und ihre Vielfalt in den Blick und widmet sich der Offenen Jugendarbeit als Raum für selbige: als Experimentierplattform, als Ortniederschwelliger Kulturvermittlung, als "Werkstatt" und als Brücke zwischen den Welten (offline - online). Die Teilnehmer*innen erwarten neue methodische Ideen für die Einrichtungen sowie konkrete Ansatzpunkte, um Jugendkulturen ressourcenorientiert zu begleiten. Mit Vorträgen, Diskussionen und praxisnahen Formaten wollen wir Wissen, Erfahrungen und Tools bündeln, die direkt in den Arbeitsalltag übertragen werden können. Objectives Ziel & Programm: Die dreitägige Veranstaltung ist eine hervorragende Gelegenheit zum Netzwerken, Kontakte knüpfen, Diskutieren und für regen Austausch von Praktiker/innen und Expert/innen aus Wissenschaft und Politik. Im Rahmen der heurigen Fachtagung wollen wir uns auf vielfältige Weise ethischen Grundsätzen in unserer professionellen Arbeit widmen. Neben spannenden Vorträgen werden interaktive Formate geboten, in denen diskutiert, reflektiert und neue Perspektiven entstehen können.

title: Harmony Within: Empowering Self Care Practices for Youth Workers
summary_en: The Harmony Within Training Course is designed to equip youth workers with the knowledge, skills, and tools to effectively support their mental wellness and follow psychological hygiene basics. This comprehensive Programme focuses on promoting positive mental health, resilience, and well-being among youth workers, while also addressing the unique challenges and pressures they face in today's society. Throughout the course, participants will explore various aspects of mental wellness, including understanding common mental health issues among young people, identifying early signs of distress, and implementing strategies for prevention and intervention. The training will cover evidence-based approaches, practical techniques, and self-care practices that can be integrated into the daily work of youth workers.

title: Social Theatre Social Media: Creating Digital Content
summary_en: Social Media Social Theatre is an Erasmus+ training activity for youth workers, trainers, facilitators, coordinators and other professionals active in non-formal learning and youth work. The training is designed for applicants who want to strengthen the quality of their work with young people by using creative, participatory and digital methods. The activity focuses on creating educational digital content through storytelling, theatre techniques and audiovisual production. Participants will explore how social theatre, creative communication and digital media can be used to address social issues, support youth learning, and make educational messages more engaging and accessible. During the training, participants will develop practical skills in scriptwriting, storyboarding, filming, sound recording, basic editing, directing and digital communication. They will work in international teams to plan and produce short educational videos or audio pieces connected to topics relevant to young people and local communities. The training combines non-formal education methods, creative workshops, teamwork, reflection and hands-on production. It aims to increase participants’ confidence in using digital tools, strengthen their communication and cooperation skills, and support them in bringing new creative methods back to their organisations and youth work practice. After the activity, participants are expected to share what they have learned with colleagues and young people, and to contribute to the wider impact of the project through follow-up activities in their local communities.

title: SoliDARE - Meeting for organisations active in ESC in programme & partner countries
summary_en: We invite you to the annual community event SoliDARE, which brings together representatives from organisations in programme and partner countries, active in the European Solidarity Corps programme and NA representatives. The event aims to consolidate and expand the community of organisations that are part of the European Solidarity Corps network. The SALTO European Solidarity Corps together with the Estonian National Agency and Romanian National Agency is organizing the internațional event “SoliDARE”, with the financial support of the Turkish, German, French, Polish, Italian & Austrian National Agencies. This is the 5th edition of a series of events that will support community building for ESC organisations, which are coordinated by the SALTO European Solidarity Corps. This community-building event in Tallinn will bring together practitioners from programme countries and partner regions to reconnect around the values of the European Solidarity Corps and the wider impact of EU volunteering. The three full working days will cover the following objectives: Bring inspiration to engage with individual and organisational change Build a sense of membership & togetherness in the ESC programme community Harvesting and structuring the learning from past and current EU volunteering programs for feeding into the next programme Allow space for networking and creation of future partnerships Target group Representatives of organizations having a Quality Label in ESC. title: Generation Lab - practice lab on intergenerational solidarity and dialogue
summary_en: Theme: The Treaty on European Union states that ‘The Union ... shall promote ... solidarity between generations’. However, ageism, negative attitudes towards certain age groups, and demographic trends paired with multiple crises are dividing European societies and creating barriers to real inclusion and participation. These issues affect not only older generations but will also affect today’s younger generations in the future. At the same time, intergenerational solidarity and positive influences on economic development would cover the needs of different generations in a sustainable way, strengthening both democracy and social cohesion. Intergenerational solidarity and dialogue are becoming central to youth work because demographic shifts, economic pressures, and political polarisation are influencing how generations see their roles in society and how they imagine the future. Youth work is one of the few fields where spaces for learning, participation, and community-building can be intentionally designed to bring different age groups into constructive interaction. Strengthening intergenerational practices in youth work is therefore directly connected to maintaining participation, trust in democratic institutions, social responsibility, and community resilience. What are intergenerational solidarity & dialogue and how can YOUth work address it is important question that we want to tackle with this practice lab. We aim to create a space for reflection and explore both “traditional” and innovative ways of building constructive dialogue between generations. This includes analysing our own perspectives, as well as the role of youth work in fostering positive interactions among individuals and groups of different ages, values, beliefs, and cultural backgrounds. This practice lab will encourage participants to examine diverse approaches to intergenerational solidarity, community building, and volunteering, while experimenting with new ideas and possibilities. Throughout th

title: “ShiftWork — Non-formal Paths to Entrepreneurship”
summary_en: This training course offers youth workers the opportunity to deepen their understanding of entrepreneurship and entrepreneurial learning within youth work and non-formal education contexts, and to strengthen their capacity to design and facilitate practical entrepreneurial learning experiences for young people. Through non-formal education and experiential learning approaches, participants will explore key concepts from the EntreComp framework — including creativity, vision, self-awareness, motivation, ethical thinking, planning, and working with others — while gaining insight into practical tools and methods that promote entrepreneurial mindsets among youth. The course places strong emphasis on the role of youth workers as facilitators of entrepreneurial learning processes. Participants will examine how educational activities can be designed and facilitated in an engaging and empowering way, with particular attention to building creative confidence, encouraging initiative, supporting young people in recognising opportunities, and connecting entrepreneurial competences to real-life youth work contexts. By combining theoretical input with structured reflection and peer exchange, the training supports participants in critically analysing their own facilitation styles, professional practices, and the entrepreneurial realities in their home countries. Going beyond theory, the training course is highly participatory and practice-oriented. Participants will engage in interactive workshops, small-group design sessions, and hands-on activities focused on applying EntreComp principles in youth work. Special focus will be placed on developing concrete educational activities and local-level session designs that integrate entrepreneurial learning into everyday youth work practice. By the end of the course, participants will have strengthened their facilitation competences, developed practical activity concepts, and gained tools and strategies to foster entrepreneurial thinking,

title: Sound Atlas of Resilience
summary_en: This training course is designed for youth workers, music educators, young musicians and facilitators who want to explore empowerment through music, music as a support tool and emotional resilience in the context of social challenges. The course will focus on how music can help individuals and communities express emotions, strengthen self-confidence, build connection and find constructive ways to respond to difficult life situations. Throughout the training, participants will reflect on the role of music as a valuable support tool in youth work and community-based practice use of music in work with refugees, migrants and other groups affected by displacement, exclusion, trauma, or social instability open discussion on how music can be connected with elements of art therapy and how creative musical processes can support emotional well-being, inclusion, and resilience. An important part of the course will be: exchanging experiences, methods and good practices related to using music in youth work. sharing own approaches and learning from one another how music can become a practical and meaningful tool for participation, communication and social support among young people. The course will also include a collaborative creative process in mixed groups, where participants will create musical backgrounds for the stories of Ukrainian refugee children. This activity will encourage participants to work with personal narratives in a sensitive and respectful way, while discovering how music can accompany stories, emotions, and experiences that are often difficult to express in words alone. By combining reflection, practice, and intercultural cooperation, the training aims to strengthen participants’ competences in using music for emotional resilience, empowerment, inclusion and social support.

title: Sustainable ImPact 3
summary_en: Sustainable ImPACT 3 Welcome to the 3rd edition of Sustainable ImPACT. While the EU has strengthened its focus on sustainability and the circular economy, overproduction and excessive waste remain persistent challenges, particularly in urban environments. To drive lasting change and support the EU Green Deal, we need to equip the younger generation with the right skills and mindset. This is where youth workers play an irreplaceable role. Who is this for? This training course is designed specifically for professionals and volunteers active in non-formal learning and youth work (youth workers, youth leaders, trainers, facilitators, and NGO staff). If you work directly with young people and want to integrate environmental awareness into your daily youth work practices, this training is for you. Taking place in Czechia, this training course focuses exclusively on urban sustainability challenges (such as waste management, fast fashion, infrastructure, and the impact of urban lifestyles). The activities are built upon the European Sustainability Competence Framework and Environmental "Onion" methodology used in Czech Environmental Education, ensuring a comprehensive and structured approach to green education. Through an interactive program, we aim to equip youth workers with crucial sustainability competencies. Participants will: Understand current urban sustainability challenges and the impact of European environmental policies. Learn how to advocate for sustainable consumption and green habits within their local communities. Adopt practical methods and apply digital tools specifically tailored for environmental education. Train to design, run, and evaluate their own sustainability activities and impactful promotional campaigns. Connect with fellow youth workers across Europe to share best practices and strengthen cross-border partnerships. The course is built entirely on the principles of non-formal education. You can expect a dynamic learning environment featuring grou
audience: Bosnia and Herzegovina, Czech Republic, France, Italy, Lithuania, Portugal, Romania, Spain

title: TEC FORUM 2026
summary_en: In 2026, we come together under the theme “Voicing the Future and Shaping Tomorrow Together” — a call to actively contribute and collaborate. The event consists of three full working days, with arrival on 7 September and departure on 11 September. Focus and Key Topics TEC Forum 2026 builds on the strong legacy of previous editions while placing a dedicated focus on this year’s theme, guided by the following topics: Including broader political and geopolitical dynamics and their impact on participants’ realities and educational challenges within TEC Discussing the strategic relevance of volunteering – and TEC in particular — in the context of the likely integration of the European Solidarity Corps into Erasmus+ Developing and pre-testing concrete approaches to address emerging European priorities in TEC planning, methodologies, and trainers’ competences Exploring safeguarding practices and peer support systems Strengthening the TEC community as a space for continuous exchange and learning What to Expect Throughout the three working days, participants will have the opportunity to: Exchange questions, insights, ideas, and experiences from diverse perspectives, including trainers, NA officers, and SALTO officers Engage in dialogue with a representative of the European Commission Learn from expert contributions on key topics Share presentations and receive feedback on tools and methods, with a focus on the main theme Collaborate to develop new solutions to challenges relevant to TEC implementation Strengthen and consolidate networks and working groups Foster a sense of belonging within the international TEC community and encourage mutual support Continue the exchange in a follow-up online meeting after the event Methodology We offer a space where participants can engage in continuous working groups aligned with the event theme throughout the three working days. These groups provide an opportunity to explore pressing issues in greater depth and share their outcomes with t
audience: Eastern Partnership countries , Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Southern Mediterranean countries , Western Balkan countries

title: Need for NEET: Online speed dating
summary_en: With this second edition of our online speed dating, we would like to once more bring together organisations working with young people in NEET situation, this time specifically focusing on the topic of mental health. If this is something that you have been concentrating on in your organization, or would like to work on more going forward, we would love to have you join us online to potentially find new project partners! What’s the goal? Expand your network: Meet like-minded organizations from across Europe working with young people in NEET situation. Exchange best practices: Share your experience, challenges, and success stories. Find new partners: Connect with potential collaborators for Erasmus+ projects and beyond. Who should attend? This event is for youth organizations, NGOs, community centers, and social enterprises that support NEET youth through nonformal education, employment programs, mentoring, street work, and other initiatives. Whether you’re experienced in international cooperation or just starting, this is your chance to meet the right partners! How does it work? Quick and structured online meeting with potential partners in small groups. Virtual networking space (please be prepared to present your organization and your work). Practical tips on funding opportunities and international cooperation. What’s in it for you? ✔ New contacts for future collaboration ✔ Inspiration for innovative youth projects ✔ A better understanding of how to engage NEET youth internationally. The activity will take place online on 10 June 2026 (15:00 to 17:00). Each organization can register only once (one registered participant). However, if multiple people gather around the computer, that’s not a problem. We are looking forward to connecting with you!
audience: Eastern Partnership countries , Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Southern Mediterranean countries , Western Balkan countries

Online Youthpass Meeting for beneficiaries
summary_en: When: 19.05.2026 at 11:30-13:00 CEST (Brussels time) Where: Online on Zoom. You will be sent the link to the Zoom meeting once you register for the workshop here. What to expect from the meeting: Presentation of Youthpass, its concept and features, as a tool that supports reflective learning in the youth work and volunteering within the EU youth programmes Tips and tricks to make the most of Youthpass in your projects More information on Youthpass: Youthpass is the recognition tool of Erasmus+ Youth and the European Solidarity Corps. The Youthpass concept, in addition to the certificate, considers it a facilitated reflective learning process that leads to self-assessment of the developed competences. The meeting is open for beneficiaries in the youth field, no matter your experience level. So, do join! Please note: Meeting will be held in English and a good level of English is expected from participants. There is no selection process, you can simply register and join the meeting. The session will not be recorded. So, don't miss this unique opportunity!
audience: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries

title: Voice and Action 4.0®️
summary_en: We will empower 30 youth workers with essential skills, efficient tools, and supportive programs in fostering youth engagement at the local and international level. Our goal is to bridge the gap between young people and democracy. Today's youth increasingly participate through alternative means such as activism, volunteering, and online movements like climate activism. As youth civil society evolves, it shifts from hierarchical structures to a more diverse and interconnected network. However, many youth civil society organizations face challenges like restrictions on their freedoms of association and expression, contributing to a 'shrinking space for civil society'. In Europe, it's crucial to recognize and support various forms of youth participation in democracy. Despite this, many young individuals still feel disconnected from democratic structures, highlighting the ongoing need for their involvement. Given the rise of extremism, promoting active citizenship and youth participation is more vital than ever. The Erasmus+ and European Solidarity Corps programs reinforce this objective. Therefore, our objectives are: 1. Enhance the skills of 30 youth workers in promoting youth participation through knowledge exchange, sharing success stories, and implementing attractive programs. 2. Raise awareness and promote active citizenship among local youth through engaging campaigns and empowerment initiatives. 3. Build the capacity of European NGOs to empower youth in local communities through collaborative campaigns and international projects. Link for info-pack - https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1pHtbtbXGfLBqtljPztb1cTxypy8H26dk?usp=drive_link

title: TOGETHER
summary_en: It will take place from 2 to 9 June in Laguépie, in south-west France, and will bring together 30 people (older than 18), including youth workers, social workers, volunteers within associations, and people involved in community projects (or wishing to join such projects in the near future). By alternating between theory and practice and drawing on a wide range of methods, we will encourage you to question your own beliefs and behaviours, to compare and contrast your perspectives, to critically analyse different situations, to put certain ideas discussed during the course to the test in real-world settings, to learn from others’ experiences, and to explore practical ways of fostering greater cooperation. This course aims to offer new perspectives on this topic, to develop participants’ ability to listen and be open to others, to explore practical ways of working towards the creation of more inclusive societies, to promote diversity and combat discrimination on the one hand, and to take action for the planet on the other. You will also gain collective experience of developing and implementing a project in collaboration with local stakeholders and the local area where the training will take place. By the end of the week, we hope you will have gained a deeper understanding of the topics covered and acquired the knowledge and inspiration needed to identify ways of acting in line with your beliefs and values.
audience: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries

title: The Entrepreneurial Process as a Learning Method
summary_en: This training course is designed for youth workers, group leaders, facilitators, and educators active in the non-formal education youth sector within the Erasmus+ programme. It is implemented under the Erasmus+ accreditation in the field of youth 2023–2027 and introduces the entrepreneurial process as a structured learning approach that strengthens key competences among young people and enhances the quality of youth work practice. The course responds to the need for practical, experience-based methods that support initiative, creativity, and active participation. The training presents the entrepreneurial process as a sequence of learning stages, including identifying needs, generating ideas, testing solutions, collaborating in teams, and reflecting on results. These stages are translated into concrete non-formal education tools that can be directly applied in youth work settings. Through this approach, participants explore ways to support the development of competences such as problem-solving, communication, teamwork, adaptability, and initiative. The programme is based on experiential learning and learning by doing, using workshops, simulations, group challenges, and reflection. Participants develop facilitation competences, including designing learning activities, guiding group processes, and ensuring inclusive participation, with attention to diverse groups and young people with fewer opportunities. By the end, they gain practical tools and confidence to apply process-based learning and support young people in turning ideas into action. Activities: Interactive Workshops: Sessions focused on process-based learning, facilitation techniques, and the development of key competences such as initiative, creativity, and problem-solving. Applied Learning Sessions: Activities where participants experience and apply the stages of the entrepreneurial process within a non-formal education context. Simulation Exercises: Group challenges and role-plays aimed at strengthening te

title: SoliDARE Meet-up #2:The lights & shadows of preparedness, 21st of May, 14:30 - 16:00 CET
summary_en: This MeetUp is a community space for those of us working with young people and organisations who are curious, questioning, and willing to look beyond the concept together. But before we go further, here is a shifting thought: We live in a world of self-regulation tools, well-being practices, and coping frameworks. We know how to breathe, how to ground ourselves, how to set goals. And yet, loneliness is rising. People are disappearing from each other's lives. We say "fine, managing" when we are not. We have many “techniques”. And we are still losing each other. Preparedness was designed as an institutional language for managing infrastructure, state continuity, and economic systems. The closer it moves towards individuals and communities, the more it risks becoming yet another obligation placed on people who are already overburdened. The intention may be communal. The effect, far too often, leads to individualisation. And here lies the deeper question this meet-up wants to hold: What if, instead of preparing for something we wish to avoid, we prepared for what we wish to create? Building on the SALTO ESC webinar Youth Volunteering & Preparedness Webinar, and the essay Open Windows, this met-up invites us to: Explore the lights where the preparedness agenda opens new possibilities for youth work and volunteering. Name the shadow, what happens when the language of readiness asks too much of individuals and too little of the structures around them? Ask the question, what is our role, as coordinators, mentors and non-formal educators, when preparedness enters our work? Continue and widen the conversation started at SALTO ESC RC, bringing in voices, experiences and doubts from across our community About the speaker: Dagna Gmitrowicz is an internationally active facilitator with many years of experience working with people and organisations. She is the co-author of the essay Open Windows, together with Eliza Zadłużna (April 2026, KulturNest e.V.), a provocation and invitat
audience: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries , Other countries in the world , Partner Countries Neighbouring the EU

title: LIFE COMPASS
summary_en: Feeling overwhelmed, out of balance, or searching for direction? LIFE COMPASS is a transformative training course designed to help you reset, reflect, and realign- both personally and professionally. This training course is designed for youth workers and professionals active in non-formal learning who wish to strengthen both their personal development and their capacity to support young people. The course offers a holistic framework based on 8 key life areas: Health Emotions Relationships Social life Career Finances Personality Vision Through non-formal education methods such as experiential learning, guided reflection and deep introspection, group work, and practical exercises, participants will explore their own balance across these life areas while gaining tools and methods that can be directly applied in youth work practice. The training creates a safe and supportive learning environment where participants can reflect on their experiences, exchange practices, and develop new approaches to working with young people. A key element of the course is the co-creation of a digital LIFE COMPASS handbook, which will include methods, exercises, and tools developed and tested during the training. Participants will also be encouraged to transfer their learning into their local contexts by implementing follow-up activities after the course. Participant profile The training is designed for 24 participants (are at least 22 years old ) who are actively working with young people in non-formal or formal settings. We are looking for: Youth workers and youth leaders Educators and teachers Social workers working with young people Volunteers and facilitators in youth organisations Project coordinators and team leaders Participants should: have experience working with young people be interested in personal development & supporting youth growth be open to experiential, deep introspection and reflection methods be motivated to apply and share the learning after the training Impact and f

summary_en: This training course brings together youth workers, youth leaders, volunteers and individuals actively involved in the field of non-formal education and youth work, with the aim of strengthening their competences in engaging young people through innovative, participatory and experiential learning approaches. The activity is designed to support the professional development of participants by providing practical tools, methods and knowledge that can be directly applied in their daily youth work practice. The training focuses on key areas such as youth engagement, volunteering, healthy lifestyles and community development, while promoting active citizenship and social responsibility among young people. Throughout the programme, participants will take part in a variety of non-formal learning activities, including interactive workshops, group work, simulations, outdoor activities and reflection sessions. The methodology is based on experiential learning, peer-to-peer exchange and intercultural dialogue, allowing participants to learn from each other’s experiences and realities in different countries. Special attention is given to creating an inclusive and supportive learning environment, where participants are encouraged to share their perspectives, develop new ideas and strengthen their competences in working with diverse groups of young people, including those with fewer opportunities. The training course also aims to strengthen international cooperation between organisations and youth workers, fostering networking, partnership building and the development of future initiatives within the Erasmus+ programme. By the end of the activity, participants will have enhanced their skills in designing and implementing non-formal education activities, improved their ability to motivate and involve young people, and gained new insights into how to contribute to the development of resilient and active communities. title: COMPASS
summary_en: COMPASS: The Core of Outdoor Mindful Practices for Adventurous and Sustainable Self Dates: 5th – 14th June 2026 Location: Jeseníky Mountains, Czechia COMPASS is an intensive, 8-day experiential training course designed to promote personal resilience and inner sustainability through Adventure Education and mental well-being. This is a digital-free, "off-the-grid" experience where participants will switch off their devices to reconnect with their senses and natural resources. Target audience: This offer is specifically targeted at youth workers, educators, mentors, NGO staff members, and leaders working in the field of non-formal learning who are looking to expand their professional toolkit. It is ideal for those motivated to re-engage passive youth, combat digital burnout, and lead with empathy. Core activities & methodology The program follows a "learning by doing" approach, combining demanding physical challenges with deep, guided reflection. Key activities include: Living on the move: Living in constant motion through the mountains, carrying essentials, and setting up camp in different natural settings each day. Physical challenges: Trekking (mountain ascent), canoeing adventures, high ropes, and a symbolic firewalk. Mindfulness & reflection: Guided debriefing, practical mindfulness, and creative mind-mapping to build crisis management skills. Shared leadership: Managing a nomadic lifestyle together, from team budget planning to shared cooking and low-impact living. Training goals: 1) Strengthen personal resilience: Building emotional and physical endurance to prevent professional burnout. 2) Master adventure education: Acquiring a professional toolkit to use adventure and nature as educational tools. 3) Crisis management: Learning to hold space for others and navigate professional or personal failures with compassion. 4) Celebrating diversity: Joining an international network to share best practices and celebrate personal uniqueness. Practical Information: Accomm

title: Home, Sweet City
summary_en: A five-day training for youth workers (people who work with young people) from Latvia and Estonia (primarily Daugavpils and Narva) on strengthening the emotional connection of young people in the border cities of Estonia and Latvia. Participants will explore the city, create creative routes and excursions, develop communication skills, and develop their own project to implement with young people. Meals are included. Participation is free, but participants must implement their own follow-up project with young people by December 1, 2026, based on the knowledge gained during the training. A small grant will be available for the project. Home Sweet City is a KA2 Small-Scale Partnership project coordinated by Multi Nutikas (Estonia) in a partnership with Miromida (Latvia). The project aims to strengthen the emotional connection of young people in the border cities of Estonia and Latvia –Narva and Daugavpils - with their hometowns and to foster their proactivity through creative exploration of the urban environment, self-presentation in public spaces, intercultural dialogue, and engagement in community life. At the same time, it expands the toolkit of youth workers by offering innovative methodologies and practical solutions to support youth initiatives, develop digital competences, and promote intercultural understanding.
audience: Estonia, Latvia

title: Gaming for climate chanCe
summary_en: "Gaming for Climate Change" is a project co-funded by the European Union under Key Action 2 (KA2) of the Erasmus+ programme. It brings together 5 organisations across 5 countries: InformaGiovani (Italy), Equação (Portugal), Ambitia (Slovenia), Share Europe (Bulgaria), and Radi Vidi Pats (Latvia). The project addresses the topic of climate change through gamification as a non-formal education methodology. More precisely, it aims to equip youth workers with effective educational tools for engaging young people on environmental issues in a fun and meaningful way. The main outcome will be a set of 4 educational games on climate change. The games will be the result of 22 months of interconnected activities, encompassing: the design and development of the games themselves; local and international testing sessions involving young people — including those with fewer opportunities — and youth workers; and promotion and dissemination activities at both local and international level, aimed at raising the project's visibility, sharing its results, and advocating for its inclusive and participatory approach. This activity centres on the hands-on testing and technical evaluation of four game prototypes — two analogue and two digital — developed as part of the project "Gaming for Climate Change". Participants will be guided through the entire development process, from the initial concept to the tools used for production. A significant part of the activity will be dedicated to direct game testing: participants will play the games together and provide feedback and professional insights as youth workers. This input will be invaluable in refining gameplay mechanics and addressing any issues before the final versions are released.

title: Talk it Out: Your Starter Kit for Real Connection
summary_en: Conflicts and misunderstandings happen everywhere - at school, university, online, in friendships, and in youth groups. But they don’t have to push people apart. In this webinar, we’ll explore how young people can turn tense moments into opportunities for understanding and connection. Joining the webinar, you’ll learn simple, practical dialogue techniques you can start using right away to support others, navigate tough conversations, and create safer spaces for communication. If you’re curious about dialogue, managing emotions, or peer mediation, this is the perfect place to begin. What you’ll get from the webinar: practical tools for guiding conversations when emotions run high insights into what makes communication productive (and what blocks it) a chance to try out core skills in an interactive, supportive setting information on how to continue learning through our free online courses Think mediation is only for professionals? We’ll show you how anyone, with the right mindset and a few practical tools, can make a real difference in their community. This webinar is part of the DEQ project, where young people and youth workers can access high‑quality, free learning programs on dialogue, empathy, conflict transformation and digital youth work. Explore more at: https://deq.digital Who is it for? Young people, youth workers, educators, volunteers, peer leaders, and anyone who wants to strengthen their communication skills. Why join? Because real connection starts with real dialogue - and you can learn how to make those conversations easier, safer, and more meaningful. Number of project ERASMUS+: DEQ-2023-2-SK02-KA220-YOU-000177746
audience: Erasmus+ Youth Programme countries

title: NowGen Volunteers NL 2026
summary_en: This year, European Volunteering is celebrating its 30th birthday. This marks three decades of young people travelling abroad and making a positive impact on society. To celebrate this milestone and gather insights for the future of European Volunteering, we are looking to connect with today’s volunteers – based on your experience, how can we ensure that European Volunteering continues to align with your motivation, needs and wishes? This activity is therefore designed to volunteers with recent transnational volunteering experience. Through creating a consultative space, this activity aims to make sure your voice will be heard and can influence the volunteering landscape in the future. We will connect specifically to topics that are currently being discussed by experts and policy makers. At the same time, we also want to inspire you to continue your solidarity journey. Participating in this activity will give you a chance to think about what that means for you and which steps you can take to transform your dreams and amibtions into actions. This activity is taking place within the framework of a partnership between 16 National Agencies and 3 SALTOs Resource Centres accross the EU who are implementing a constellation of activities aiming at advocating for and creating better conditions for EU Youth Volunteering in Europe and beyond. This event is a unique opportunity for former volunteers to influence the work of this partnership of National Agencies and SALTOs. Key objectives of the activity: Exchange experiences with peers from different volunteering schemes about the impact of your volunteering activities, the obstacles you faced and the way you contributed to the community. Enhance the potential of European Volunteering by sharing concrete feedback and propositions for change and contribute to the work of organisations, National Agencies, SALTOs and the European Commission. Explore how to make your volunteering journey last—personally and socially Methodology To

title: Rural.exe
summary_en: Rural.exe – Innovative AI Tools for Community Impact is an Erasmus+ training course designed for youth workers, educators, and active young people working in the field of non-formal learning and youth work. The training focuses on exploring how digital tools and artificial intelligence can be used to strengthen youth participation, support rural development, and create meaningful impact in local communities. Participants will gain practical skills and hands-on experience with accessible AI tools, while critically reflecting on their use in youth work and community initiatives. The programme is based on non-formal education methods, including interactive workshops, group work, simulations, and peer learning. A key element of the training is the co-creation process, where participants work in international teams to design and test their own educational activities related to AI, digitalisation, and rural challenges. These activities will be implemented during the training and later adapted for local follow-up initiatives. The training also places emphasis on inclusion, supporting participants from rural and underrepresented backgrounds, and encouraging the active involvement of youth workers working with fewer opportunities. By the end of the activity, participants will: develop practical competences in using AI tools in youth work strengthen their ability to design and facilitate non-formal learning activities exchange good practices and experiences from different countries gain inspiration and tools to implement local activities with their communities After the training, participants will be expected to organise a follow-up activity at the local level, ensuring the transfer of knowledge and wider dissemination of project results. In the Infopack, you can find the list of organisations involved in the project, which you should contact if you are interested in taking part in the project. title: Online Conference • 26 May 2026 • 17:00-18:00 CET • Rewriting the Story • Erasmus+ KA2 • Empowering Youth Against Bullying • Storytelling and Gamified Approaches for Bullying Prevention in Youth Work
summary_en: Story-based Methods for Bullying Prevention in Youth Work The E-learning Webinar Online Conference will take place on 26 May 2026 at 17:00 CET via Google Meet. https://meet.google.com/fau-oons-iao This online training activity is part of the Erasmus+ project “Rewriting the Story: Empowering Youth against Bullying”. It aims to support youth workers and educators in strengthening their competences to prevent and address bullying through innovative non-formal learning approaches. Bullying remains a significant challenge across Europe, affecting young people’s well-being, social development, and participation. Many youth workers and educators face difficulties in engaging young people on this topic and lack practical, innovative tools to address it effectively. This e-learning activity introduces participants to storytelling and gamified methods as powerful tools to explore bullying, foster empathy, and develop key social competences such as communication, assertiveness, and conflict resolution. Participants will discover how to create safe and engaging learning environments where young people can reflect on their experiences, share perspectives, and develop positive behaviours. The activity will take place online (webinar format). Practical details and access information will be shared with selected participants. It’s a Love Story - Living Nonviolent Communication (NVC) in Youth Work
summary_en: This training is designed for people who already have experience with Nonviolent Communication (NVC) and want to deepen their ability to live it in everyday reality. Many participants come to NVC trainings, feel inspired, and connect deeply with its values. Yet when returning to daily life: fast pace, conflicts, emotional pressure, it often becomes challenging to stay connected and apply NVC in practice. This training focuses exactly on this gap: moving from understanding NVC to embodying it. The programme offers an immersive 10 day learning space based on non-formal education methods, where participants will engage in practical exercises, real-life scenarios, reflection, and peer learning. There will be a focus on applying NVC in challenging situations, strengthening emotional awareness, and building resilience to remain connected when disconnection happens. Participants will explore: how to express themselves honestly while staying connected how to listen with empathy, especially in moments of tension how to navigate emotions in a constructive way how to stay connected to their own needs and prevent burnout how to integrate NVC into their daily work with young people The training also includes embodied practices (such as grounding and awareness exercises), role plays, and group processes that support learning through experience rather than theory alone. The training is designed to respond to the needs of participants, so the programme will be adapted to the challenges you face when applying NVC in your work and daily life. Participants are also invited to facilitate sessions and receive feedback from other NVC practitioners to further develop their facilitation skills. Participants profile: this activity is specifically targeted at people working in the field of non-formal learning and youth work, including youth workers, educators, facilitators, and trainers who have at least 25h+ of previous NVC training and who are applying it but struggling in some areas. By t

title: Online ESC Community of Practices in South-Med - 2026
summary_en: SALTO EuroMed is looking for participants to its future South-Med Volunteering Community of Practices. This year, the CoP will focus on the impact of volunteering on the organisations, the communities and the volunteers. The members of the CoP will be actively engaged in discussing approaches to create and assess impact of volunteering schemes on different levels. The members are asked to take an active role in defining, assessing, and eventually disseminating practices & approaches connected to local realities. This is not a passive learning space, it is a working group of practitioners who will: Share experiences Reflect critically on current volunteering practices Co-create practical outputs that can be used in daily work The objectives of the CoP are: To develop a shared understanding of volunteering impact on young people, organisations, and communities in the Euro-Med context. To co-create a practical tool to assess and strengthen the impact of volunteering, based on real practices. To foster Euro-Med cooperation by connecting practitioners and encouraging future partnerships and joint initiatives. What will be the topics: Impact on young people: skills, empowerment, inclusion Impact on organisations: capacity-building and sustainability Impact on local communities: relevance and long-term change Euro-Med dynamics: partnerships, equity, and mobility What will be the outcome: A practical resource (toolkit / framework / guidelines) Concrete case studies A network for future collaboration There will be 5 online meetings of 2 hours, attendance to all the meetings is requested for the CoP to reach its aim. summary_en: Training Course 14-22 June 2026 | Caprarola Viterbo/Rome FCO Italy International call for participants from The Netherlands for an Erasmus+ Training Course " Music as a tool for youth growth " in Viterbo, Italy. MUSIC IS A TOOL FOR YOUTH GROWTH Through a Training Course, partners from European countries receive practical tools and music-based exercises to work effectively with groups of young people, supporting their personal and social development while also promoting individual well-being. Throughout the course, participants will engage in practical activities such as body percussion and circular music, which provide immediately applicable tools for educational youth work. Project objectives: To enrich the youth workers’ toolbox with music-based educational tools that support young people’s personal, social, and cultural development. To promote educational innovation within the partner organisations by providing musical methodologies that 2 can inspire new learning pathways. To support the development of soft skills among young people — such as focus, commitment, active listening, discipline, and cooperation — through non-formal musical tools. Activities and methods: Emphasis on the use of musical methods applied to the youth sector. Body percussion and circular music will be the main tools. Active listening, collaboration, respect for others, inclusion of diversity, and non-verbal communication Choral sessions that will serve as a powerful educational tool that breaks down linguistic, cultural, and cognitive barriers Singing and music making of popular and folk songs from the participating countries` Discover and appreciate the musical cultural heritage of each country. Participants’ profile Age: 18+, no upper limit Background: Youth workers, trainers, youth leaders, volunteers, project managers, NGO staff, mentors, coaches, or peer group leaders and young people +18 at their first experiences in NFL education.
